sections.heading: Modern contraception increased, but total contraceptive use barely changed
sections.body: Among currently married women, use of any modern contraceptive method rose from 41.8 percent in 2022 to 44.5 percent in 2025. Use of any method was almost unchanged at 58.6 percent. The increase mainly came from implants, injectables, male condoms, and the pill, while female sterilization and IUD use were lower than in 2022. Compared with the all-women view, where modern use was 25.7 percent in 2025, this page shows higher levels for major modern methods because it focuses on currently married women and reports sexually active unmarried women separately.

sections.heading: High modern-use regions are clustered outside NCR
sections.body: Davao Region had the highest modern contraceptive use among currently married women at 58.2 percent. It was followed by MIMAROPA at 53.9 percent, Caraga at 52.9 percent, and SOCCSKSARGEN at 50.8 percent. These high-use regions are mostly outside the largest metropolitan center, while NCR was lower at 41.6 percent. The regional pattern is similar in the all-women view, where Davao Region, MIMAROPA, SOCCSKSARGEN, and Caraga are also among the highest, so the pattern is not only caused by which denominator is used.

sections.heading: BARMM is still far below other regions
sections.body: BARMM was far below every other region in 2025, with 20.2 percent of currently married women using a modern method. The next-lowest regions were Bicol Region at 38.4 percent, Central Visayas at 39.3 percent, and Zamboanga Peninsula¹ at 40.4 percent. The gap between Davao Region and BARMM was 38.0 points, making the spread across regions much wider than most gaps by background characteristic.

sections.heading: Some regions rose while others fell
sections.body: The national increase in modern use hides large regional shifts between 2022 and 2025. MIMAROPA rose 8.8 points and Davao Region rose 8.4 points. In contrast, Cagayan Valley fell 9.9 points and Zamboanga Peninsula¹ fell 7.5 points. This was not a simple nationwide increase: some regions moved higher, while others lost ground from already high 2022 levels.

sections.heading: Regions reach modern-use totals through different methods
sections.body: Regions can have similar modern-use totals but rely on very different methods. Davao Region led pill use at 29.7 percent. MIMAROPA led injectables at 12.6 percent and implants at 9.2 percent, while CAR had the highest female sterilization at 17.6 percent. This matters because a high modern-use region may depend more on short-acting methods, long-acting reversible methods, or permanent methods.

sections.heading: Modern use is highest in the middle ages
sections.body: Among currently married women, modern contraceptive use was highest at ages 35-39, at 48.7 percent. It was almost as high at ages 30-34, at 48.3 percent. Use was lower among women aged 15-19 at 34.0 percent and women aged 45-49 at 33.9 percent. This means the pattern is not a steady rise with age. Instead, use levels off in the middle ages, with younger and older married women using modern methods less often.

sections.heading: Modern use jumps after women have children
sections.body: By number of living children, modern use was lowest among currently married women with no children, at 7.6 percent. It was much higher among those with 1-2 children, at 42.1 percent. It peaked among women with 3-4 children at 59.1 percent, then fell to 49.3 percent among women with five or more children. The biggest contrast is not between groups with many children, but between women with no children and women already raising children.

sections.heading: Rural use is higher, but the gap is modest
sections.body: Currently married women in rural areas had higher modern contraceptive use than those in urban areas: 47.2 percent compared with 42.1 percent. Both residence groups increased from 2022. Rural use rose from 44.3 percent, and urban use rose from 39.7 percent. The rural-urban gap matters, but it is much smaller than the gap between the highest and lowest regions.

sections.heading: More schooling does not always mean higher modern use
sections.body: Modern contraceptive use was highest among currently married women with Grades 7-10 education, at 48.3 percent. It was close behind among women with Grades 1-6 at 46.7 percent and Grades 11-12 at 46.6 percent. Use was lower among women with college education at 39.3 percent and no education at 34.8 percent. The pattern does not show a simple link where more schooling always means higher modern use.

sections.heading: Modern use is higher in lower wealth groups
sections.body: The wealth pattern moves downward rather than upward. Modern use was 48.6 percent in the lowest quintile and 48.2 percent in the second quintile, compared with 37.7 percent in the highest quintile. The fourth quintile had the largest increase from 2022, rising from 34.8 percent to 42.6 percent. This shows that wealth alone does not create a simple pattern of higher modern contraceptive use among currently married women.

sections.heading: Sexually active unmarried women use a different mix of methods
sections.body: Among sexually active unmarried women, modern contraceptive use was 26.2 percent in 2025, up from 23.8 percent in 2022. At the same time, use of any method fell from 41.4 percent to 37.9 percent. Pill use more than doubled, from 7.1 percent to 15.3 percent, while male condom use fell from 11.9 percent to 6.6 percent. This differs from currently married women, who have higher modern use overall and much larger shares using female sterilization, injectables, and implants.

sections.heading: Residence matters more for method choice than overall use
sections.body: For sexually active unmarried women, modern use was almost the same in rural and urban areas: 26.7 percent in rural areas and 26.0 percent in urban areas. The method mix differed more than the overall level. Rural male condom use was 10.2 percent compared with 4.9 percent in urban areas, while urban pill use was slightly higher at 15.8 percent compared with 14.4 percent. Residence is therefore a weak divider for overall modern use in this group, but it still matters for which methods women report.

takeaways: Modern contraceptive use among currently married women rose to 44.5 percent in 2025, with gains focused on selected reversible methods rather than every method.
Regional differences are the sharpest divide: Davao Region, MIMAROPA, Caraga, and SOCCSKSARGEN are high-use areas, while BARMM remains a clear low outlier.
Sexually active unmarried women have lower modern use at 26.2 percent, with a clear shift toward pills and away from male condoms between 2022 and 2025.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
